summary: Gruber speculates on whether Apple will hold another keynote event in late 2019, noting that the window for October invitations has essentially closed. He identifies three expected products — Mac Pro, 16-inch MacBook Pro, and noise-cancelling AirPods — but argues they don't form a cohesive narrative worthy of a keynote. He suggests Apple may instead opt for private media briefings, as they did in 2017 for the iMac Pro. He concludes that Apple is likely too busy with the TV+ launch on November 1 to orchestrate another event.
key_points: The timing window for an October keynote has likely passed based on historical invitation patterns
Three products are expected before year-end — Mac Pro, 16-inch MacBook Pro with scissor keyboard, and AirPods Pro — but they lack a cohesive event narrative
The MacBook Pro's most interesting feature (the keyboard fix) is something Apple wouldn't want to highlight on stage, as it draws attention to the butterfly keyboard's failures
Apple's focus on the November 1 TV+ launch and premiere events makes another keynote logistically unlikely
Private media briefings would let Apple stagger announcements rather than forcing unrelated products into one event
money_quotes: Apple doesn't hold events willy-nilly just because there's something new. They tell stories at events. There's a narrative flow to them.
AirPods, a slightly bigger MacBook Pro, and a Mac Pro that was unveiled in June at WWDC don't make for an event.
And the most interesting thing about the new MacBook Pro — the keyboard — isn't something Apple would want to talk about on stage.

Updated AirPods are something Apple would ideally want to announce sooner rather than later, to make them available for holiday gift purchases. Mac Pros and high-end MacBook Pros aren't holiday gifts.
I fully expect more Apple hardware before the end of the year, but not another keynote event.
key_insight: Apple's keynote events are storytelling exercises with narrative coherence, and when the remaining product lineup doesn't form a compelling story, Apple opts for quieter private briefings instead.
